texts.label: text
texts.orig: Anthropological CO2 is considered as the primary greenhouse gas which results in climate change because of its large emission and chemical stability (Cuffey and Vimeux, 2001). Consequently, a lot of ecological impacts e.g., from polar terrestrial to tropical marine environments, and from the species to the community levels have emerged (Walther et al., 2002). With the rapid development of economics, fossil fuel, as a main energy supply, has been intensively consumed which boosted the fossil fuel-related CO2 emission from 15 Gton in 1970 to 32 Gton in 2010 over the world (Edenhofer et al., 2014). China emitted 9860 Mton of CO2 in 2012 (Olivier et al., 2012). And the coal-fired power plant accounted for 48.7% of the total CO2 emission in China. Chinese government committed to reduce the CO2 emission by 40-45% in 2020 compared to 2005. Thus, it is critical to develop efficient and economic technologies for CO2 capture from the coal-fired power plant in China (Merkel et al., 2010). In general, CO2 capture can be cataloged into post-combustion and pre-combustion configurations. For most of the existing conventional coal-fired power plants and

Thus, it is critical to develop efficient and economic technologies for CO2 capture from the coal-fired power plant in China (Merkel et al., 2010). texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: a b s t r a c t
texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Advanced solvents are under development to reduce the energy consumption for CO2 capture from the coal-fired power plant. In this work, an amino acid, sodium glycinate (SG), and an ionic liquid, [Bmim][BF4], were blended to be used as solvent for CO2 capture. The parametric tests such as blending mole ratio, temperature, CO2 partial pressure and oxygen concentration were investigated. Results revealed the highest CO2 absorption rate was achieved when the blending mole ratio of SG and [Bmim][BF4] was 4:1. Compared with the pure SG solution, the CO2 absorption rate into those blends was 40% higher than that into the pure SG solution under the same tested conditions. The high temperature and CO2 partial pressure facilitated the CO2 absorption. Overall, the blends of SG and [Bmim][BF4] possessing a high CO2 absorption rate, good anti-oxidation ability, and good regeneration efficiency may be an alternative of the MEA solution for CO2 capture.
texts.text: Advanced solvents are under development to reduce the energy consumption for CO2 capture from the coal-fired power plant. In this work, an amino acid, sodium glycinate (SG), and an ionic liquid, [Bmim][BF4], were blended to be used as solvent for CO2 capture. The parametric tests such as blending mole ratio, temperature, CO2 partial pressure and oxygen concentration were investigated. Results revealed the highest CO2 absorption rate was achieved when the blending mole ratio of SG and [Bmim][BF4] was 4:1. Compared with the pure SG solution, the CO2 absorption rate into those blends was 40% higher than that into the pure SG solution under the same tested conditions. The high temperature and CO2 partial pressure facilitated the CO2 absorption. Overall, the blends of SG and [Bmim][BF4] possessing a high CO2 absorption rate, good anti-oxidation ability, and good regeneration efficiency may be an alternative of the MEA solution for CO2 capture.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: most industrial combustion processes, the post-combustion carbon capture is probably the preferable choice in the near-term because it requires the minimum retrofit of the existing facility (Rochelle, 2009).
texts.text: most industrial combustion processes, the post-combustion carbon capture is probably the preferable choice in the near-term because it requires the minimum retrofit of the existing facility (Rochelle, 2009).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the past several decades, absorption, adsorption, and membrane-based separation have been investigated for the postcombustion CO2 capture (Mondal et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2011; Wilcox et al., 2014; Yu et al., 2012). Among these technologies, amine-based absorption process is proposed to be the most applicable technology for CO2 capture before 2030 (Rochelle, 2009). However, the MEA solution possesses the disadvantage such as low carbon dioxide loading capacity (kg CO2 absorbed per kg absorbent), high equipment corrosion rate, amine degradation by SO2, NO2, HCl and HF, and oxygen in flue gas which induces a high absorbent makeup rate, and high energy consumption in regeneration. Based on the U.S. department of energy (DOE) estimation, the deployment of the currently available MEA-based technologies on a new supercritical pulverized coal-fired power plant would increase the cost of electricity (COE) by ∼ 75% for a 90% CO2 capture scenario (DOE/NETL, 2011). To make the plants equipped with post-combustion carbon capture competitive with conventional coal-fired facilities, the DOE's programs devoted to reduce the overall economic penalty imparted by current MEA-based technologies by 55% which is equivalent to no more than a 35% increase in COE of an identical plant without carbon capture for the 90% CO2 capture scenario (DOE/NETL, 2011).
texts.text: In the past several decades, absorption, adsorption, and membrane-based separation have been investigated for the postcombustion CO2 capture (Mondal et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2011; Wilcox et al., 2014; Yu et al., 2012). Among these technologies, amine-based absorption process is proposed to be the most applicable technology for CO2 capture before 2030 (Rochelle, 2009). However, the MEA solution possesses the disadvantage such as low carbon dioxide loading capacity (kg CO2 absorbed per kg absorbent), high equipment corrosion rate, amine degradation by SO2, NO2, HCl and HF, and oxygen in flue gas which induces a high absorbent makeup rate, and high energy consumption in regeneration. Based on the U.S. department of energy (DOE) estimation, the deployment of the currently available MEA-based technologies on a new supercritical pulverized coal-fired power plant would increase the cost of electricity (COE) by ∼ 75% for a 90% CO2 capture scenario (DOE/NETL, 2011). To make the plants equipped with post-combustion carbon capture competitive with conventional coal-fired facilities, the DOE's programs devoted to reduce the overall economic penalty imparted by current MEA-based technologies by 55% which is equivalent to no more than a 35% increase in COE of an identical plant without carbon capture for the 90% CO2 capture scenario (DOE/NETL, 2011).

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: W. Li et al. / International Journal of Greenhouse Gas Control 42 (2015) 400-404
texts.text: W. Li et al. / International Journal of Greenhouse Gas Control 42 (2015) 400-404

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It should be noted that the energy penalty because of steam extraction for CO2-loaded MEA regeneration accounts for 60-70% of the total costs (Research and Development Solution, 2010). In general, the energy consumption for the solvent regeneration comprises of the reaction heat, the sensible heat, and the stripping heat. Recently, ionic liquids (ILs) are used as a solvent for CO2 absorption to reduce the reaction heat and stripping heat because the CO2 absorption into ILs is a physical process and the ILs possesses a low vapor pressure (Zhang et al., 2012). However, the low CO2 absorption capacity and the high viscosity, which increases the sensible heat and the energy cost to transport the solvent, make the ILs less economical compared with the current benchmark MEA process. The task specific ILs and the hybrid solvent of ILs and amines are being under development to increase the CO2 absorption capacity as well as decrease the viscosity and hence to reduce the cost of ILs-based process (Kumar et al., 2014). The hybrid solvent coupling advantages of amines and ILs may provide a better solution for post-combustion CO2 capture (Camper et al., 2008). In our previous work, MEA and [Bmim]BF4 (IL) blends were assessed to improve the CO2 absorption capacity (Lu et al., 2013). Results showed that the ILs and MEA blends of CO2 absorption performed better than their individual counterparts. In addition, the hybrid solvents exhibited higher stability and antioxidant activity compared with aqueous MEA solution. The efficient CO2 absorption by the ILs-MEA blends was also confirmed by Camper et al. (2008).
texts.text: It should be noted that the energy penalty because of steam extraction for CO2-loaded MEA regeneration accounts for 60-70% of the total costs (Research and Development Solution, 2010). In general, the energy consumption for the solvent regeneration comprises of the reaction heat, the sensible heat, and the stripping heat. Recently, ionic liquids (ILs) are used as a solvent for CO2 absorption to reduce the reaction heat and stripping heat because the CO2 absorption into ILs is a physical process and the ILs possesses a low vapor pressure (Zhang et al., 2012). However, the low CO2 absorption capacity and the high viscosity, which increases the sensible heat and the energy cost to transport the solvent, make the ILs less economical compared with the current benchmark MEA process. The task specific ILs and the hybrid solvent of ILs and amines are being under development to increase the CO2 absorption capacity as well as decrease the viscosity and hence to reduce the cost of ILs-based process (Kumar et al., 2014). The hybrid solvent coupling advantages of amines and ILs may provide a better solution for post-combustion CO2 capture (Camper et al., 2008). In our previous work, MEA and [Bmim]BF4 (IL) blends were assessed to improve the CO2 absorption capacity (Lu et al., 2013). Results showed that the ILs and MEA blends of CO2 absorption performed better than their individual counterparts. In addition, the hybrid solvents exhibited higher stability and antioxidant activity compared with aqueous MEA solution. The efficient CO2 absorption by the ILs-MEA blends was also confirmed by Camper et al. (2008).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Considering the corrosion and degradation of alkanolamines, we proposed here to use sodium glycinate (SG) and ILs blends for CO2 capture. Compared with alkanolamines, SG possesses high thermal and chemical stability. SG has the same amphions as alkanolamines in aqueous solution and thus can be expected to have a comparable CO2 absorption reactivity and capacity with the alkanolamines (Song et al., 2006). Moreover, the non-volatility and the nontoxicity of SG make amino acid salt a better choice to realize green environmental protection. Song et al. (2006) have measured the absorption equilibria data of CO2 into SG solution at the mole concentration of 10% to 30%, proving the absorption capacity of SG toward is better than that of MEA and AMP.
texts.text: Considering the corrosion and degradation of alkanolamines, we proposed here to use sodium glycinate (SG) and ILs blends for CO2 capture. Compared with alkanolamines, SG possesses high thermal and chemical stability. SG has the same amphions as alkanolamines in aqueous solution and thus can be expected to have a comparable CO2 absorption reactivity and capacity with the alkanolamines (Song et al., 2006). Moreover, the non-volatility and the nontoxicity of SG make amino acid salt a better choice to realize green environmental protection. Song et al. (2006) have measured the absorption equilibria data of CO2 into SG solution at the mole concentration of 10% to 30%, proving the absorption capacity of SG toward is better than that of MEA and AMP.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, the CO2 absorption performance of SG and ionic liquid [Bmim]BF4 hybrid solvent was investigated. The blending mole ratio of SG and [Bmim]BF4 was optimized to achieved the best performance of CO2 absorption. The parametric tests of CO2 absorption using the optimized blending ratio were also conducted. In addition, the regeneration efficiency of the SG-IL blends was evaluated.
texts.text: In this work, the CO2 absorption performance of SG and ionic liquid [Bmim]BF4 hybrid solvent was investigated. The blending mole ratio of SG and [Bmim]BF4 was optimized to achieved the best performance of CO2 absorption. The parametric tests of CO2 absorption using the optimized blending ratio were also conducted. In addition, the regeneration efficiency of the SG-IL blends was evaluated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sodium glycinate was purchased from Yotech Fine Chemical Co. Ltd. of Suzhou, China, [Bmim]BF4 from Shanghai Cheng Jie Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China, CO2 (>99.99%), O2 (>99.99%) and N2 (>99.99%) from Zhejiang Jin-gong Gas Co., China. All other chemicals were analytical grade, commercially available and used without additional purification.
texts.text: Sodium glycinate was purchased from Yotech Fine Chemical Co. Ltd. of Suzhou, China, [Bmim]BF4 from Shanghai Cheng Jie Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China, CO2 (>99.99%), O2 (>99.99%) and N2 (>99.99%) from Zhejiang Jin-gong Gas Co., China. All other chemicals were analytical grade, commercially available and used without additional purification.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Set-up of CO2 absorption and desorption
texts.text: 2.2. Set-up of CO2 absorption and desorption

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A schematic diagram of CO2 absorption and desorption set-up is shown in Fig. 1. A double-stirred cell reactor with a volume of 500 cm 3 was used for CO2 absorption tests in this work. The reactor consists of the lower flange on the liquid side and the higher flange on the gas side. The liquid and gas phases were stirred at a rate of 100 rpm and 250 rpm, respectively. An in-house made regeneration system was used for solvent regeneration tests. A three-neck flask with a total volume of 500 mL was used as a reactor for solvent
texts.text: A schematic diagram of CO2 absorption and desorption set-up is shown in Fig. 1. A double-stirred cell reactor with a volume of 500 cm 3 was used for CO2 absorption tests in this work. The reactor consists of the lower flange on the liquid side and the higher flange on the gas side. The liquid and gas phases were stirred at a rate of 100 rpm and 250 rpm, respectively. An in-house made regeneration system was used for solvent regeneration tests. A three-neck flask with a total volume of 500 mL was used as a reactor for solvent

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 1. Schematic diagram for CO2 absorption and desorption set-up.
texts.text: Fig. 1. Schematic diagram for CO2 absorption and desorption set-up.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: regeneration. An oil bath was used to provide the heat for solvent regeneration and kept the regeneration temperature constant. The moisture in the stripped CO2 was condensed and dried by a condenser and sulfuric acid solution. The flow rate of dried CO2 was determined by a soap-membrane flowmeter. A detailed description of these two set-ups can be found in our previous work (Lu et al., 2013).
texts.text: regeneration. An oil bath was used to provide the heat for solvent regeneration and kept the regeneration temperature constant. The moisture in the stripped CO2 was condensed and dried by a condenser and sulfuric acid solution. The flow rate of dried CO2 was determined by a soap-membrane flowmeter. A detailed description of these two set-ups can be found in our previous work (Lu et al., 2013).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In a typical test of CO2 absorption, 250 mL of 1 mol L -1 of SG and [Bmim]BF4 blend at a mole ratio of 8:2 was added into the double-stirred cell reactor. 1 L min -1 of gas stream containing 15% (v/v) CO2 balanced with N2 consciously flew into the headspace of the reactor. The CO2 absorption occurred at the gas-liquid interface under 30 ◦ C. The cell reactor was kept at constant temperature with the help of a water bath. The absorption test eased when the inlet and outlet CO2 concentration was equal. The effects of blending mole ratio of SG and [Bmim]BF4, temperature, CO2 partial pressure, and the oxygen concentration on the CO2 absorption performance were also determined.
texts.text: In a typical test of CO2 absorption, 250 mL of 1 mol L -1 of SG and [Bmim]BF4 blend at a mole ratio of 8:2 was added into the double-stirred cell reactor. 1 L min -1 of gas stream containing 15% (v/v) CO2 balanced with N2 consciously flew into the headspace of the reactor. The CO2 absorption occurred at the gas-liquid interface under 30 ◦ C. The cell reactor was kept at constant temperature with the help of a water bath. The absorption test eased when the inlet and outlet CO2 concentration was equal. The effects of blending mole ratio of SG and [Bmim]BF4, temperature, CO2 partial pressure, and the oxygen concentration on the CO2 absorption performance were also determined.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the solvent regeneration tests, the blending solvent was regenerated at 102 ◦ C. 250 mL of the CO2-loaded blending solvent was added into a three-neck flask. The oil batch which provided the heat of regeneration was preheated to 102 ◦ C. The moisture in the stripped CO2 gas was removed by a condenser and a sulfuric acid solution. The flow rate of the dried CO2 was measured by a soapmembrane flowmeter. The regenerated solvent was recirculated to the double-stirred cell reactor for the reuse of the CO2 absorption.
texts.text: In the solvent regeneration tests, the blending solvent was regenerated at 102 ◦ C. 250 mL of the CO2-loaded blending solvent was added into a three-neck flask. The oil batch which provided the heat of regeneration was preheated to 102 ◦ C. The moisture in the stripped CO2 gas was removed by a condenser and a sulfuric acid solution. The flow rate of the dried CO2 was measured by a soapmembrane flowmeter. The regenerated solvent was recirculated to the double-stirred cell reactor for the reuse of the CO2 absorption.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2 concentration was measured by a gas chromatography (GC-7890, Agilent, USA) equipped with Agilent PLTQ (30 m × 320 𝛍 m × 25 𝛍 m) column and TCD detector. The temperatures of the PLTQ column, sample injector, and detector are 40, 250, and 300 ◦ C, respectively. The CO2 absorption capacity was calculated by:

The temperatures of the PLTQ column, sample injector, and detector are 40, 250, and 300 ◦ C, respectively. The CO2 absorption capacity was calculated by:

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Effect of the solvent blending mole ratio
texts.text: 3.1. Effect of the solvent blending mole ratio

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The effect of solvent blending mole ratio was investigated to achieve the best CO2 absorption performance for the blends. The CO2 absorption was performed at a temperature of 30 ◦ C with 1 mol L -1 of SG and [Bmim]BF4 blends at a various mole ratio of 1:0, 8:2, 7:3, 6:4, 5:5, and 4:6. As shown in Fig. 2, the CO2 absorption capacity increased with the increase of the mole ratio of SG and [Bmim]BF4 blends. The higher the concentration of SG in the blend was, the higher the CO2 absorption capacity achieved. The amount of CO2 absorbed increased gradually with the operation time. The slope of the curve as shown in Fig. 2 revealed the absorption rates of CO2 into the tested solutions. It can be noted that the absorption rate of CO2 into all the tested solutions decreased with the operation time.
texts.text: The effect of solvent blending mole ratio was investigated to achieve the best CO2 absorption performance for the blends. The CO2 absorption was performed at a temperature of 30 ◦ C with 1 mol L -1 of SG and [Bmim]BF4 blends at a various mole ratio of 1:0, 8:2, 7:3, 6:4, 5:5, and 4:6. As shown in Fig. 2, the CO2 absorption capacity increased with the increase of the mole ratio of SG and [Bmim]BF4 blends. The higher the concentration of SG in the blend was, the higher the CO2 absorption capacity achieved. The amount of CO2 absorbed increased gradually with the operation time. The slope of the curve as shown in Fig. 2 revealed the absorption rates of CO2 into the tested solutions. It can be noted that the absorption rate of CO2 into all the tested solutions decreased with the operation time.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Both chemical and physical absorption occurred during CO2 absorption into the blends. CO2 absorption into SG was similar to that into alkanolamine, and the reactions can be expressed as following (Kumar et al., 2003):
texts.text: Both chemical and physical absorption occurred during CO2 absorption into the blends. CO2 absorption into SG was similar to that into alkanolamine, and the reactions can be expressed as following (Kumar et al., 2003):

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At the beginning stage, the chemical absorption was dominated in the blends and thus resulted in a high CO2 absorption rate. As the reaction proceeded, the SG concentration in the blends decreased as the CO2 was absorbed. Correspondingly, the absorption rate declined steadily. The similar result was obtained in some other researches using a different blended solvent (Hasib-ur-Rahman and Larachi, 2012). When the SG in the blends was almost exhausted, physical absorption, which had a far slower rate compared with

As the reaction proceeded, the SG concentration in the blends decreased as the CO2 was absorbed. Correspondingly, the absorption rate declined steadily. The similar result was obtained in some other researches using a different blended solvent (Hasib-ur-Rahman and Larachi, 2012). When the SG in the blends was almost exhausted, physical absorption, which had a far slower rate compared with

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 3. Comparison of CO2 absorption rate between the blend and their individual counterparts. (Temperature: 30 ◦ C, CO2: 15% (v/v), flow rate of the gas: 1 L min -1 , volume of the solvent: 250 mL.)
texts.text: Fig. 3. Comparison of CO2 absorption rate between the blend and their individual counterparts. (Temperature: 30 ◦ C, CO2: 15% (v/v), flow rate of the gas: 1 L min -1 , volume of the solvent: 250 mL.)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: chemical absorption, played a main role in the blends. In general, the absorption rate of CO2 into the blends decreased with the decease of the blending ratio (Fig. 2). It should be noted that the overall absorption rate of CO2 into the blend mainly depended on the concentration of SG as it absorbed CO2 via fast chemical absorption. With the decrease of the blending ratio of SG and [Bmim]BF4, the concentration of SG in the blends decreased and hence resulted in a decline of the absorption rate of CO2. The blend at the mole ratio of 7:3 and 8:2 presented a comparable CO2 absorption rate with the pure SG solution at the first 20 min, indicating that the addition of [Bmim]BF4 could promote the CO2 absorption into SG. Among the tested blends, the blend with a mole ratio of 8:2 achieved the highest CO2 absorption capacity, indicating the blend with a mole ratio of 8:2 was the optimal condition for CO2 absorption.
texts.text: chemical absorption, played a main role in the blends. In general, the absorption rate of CO2 into the blends decreased with the decease of the blending ratio (Fig. 2). It should be noted that the overall absorption rate of CO2 into the blend mainly depended on the concentration of SG as it absorbed CO2 via fast chemical absorption. With the decrease of the blending ratio of SG and [Bmim]BF4, the concentration of SG in the blends decreased and hence resulted in a decline of the absorption rate of CO2. The blend at the mole ratio of 7:3 and 8:2 presented a comparable CO2 absorption rate with the pure SG solution at the first 20 min, indicating that the addition of [Bmim]BF4 could promote the CO2 absorption into SG. Among the tested blends, the blend with a mole ratio of 8:2 achieved the highest CO2 absorption capacity, indicating the blend with a mole ratio of 8:2 was the optimal condition for CO2 absorption.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Interaction of SG and [Bmim]BF4 for CO2 absorption
texts.text: 3.2. Interaction of SG and [Bmim]BF4 for CO2 absorption

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 3 shows the CO2 absorption rate into the blends and the corresponding individual solvents. Results indicate that the absorption rate of CO2 into the blends and the pure SG solution was one order magnitude higher than that into the [Bmim]BF4 solution. Compared with the pure SG solution, the blend with the same amount of SG exhibited a higher absorption rate of CO2. The CO2 absorption rate into the blends was 40% higher than that into the pure SG solution under the same tested condition. Moreover, the CO2 absorption into the blends was 20% higher than the sum of that into the individual solvent, indicating that the [Bmim]BF4 facilitated the CO2 absorption into the SG. Our previous work revealed that the [Bmim]BF4 had a base catalytic effect on the hydration of CO2 (Lu et al., 2013). Meanwhile, the [Bmim]BF4 may be also served as a base catalysis for the deprotonation of the zwitterion.
texts.text: Fig. 3 shows the CO2 absorption rate into the blends and the corresponding individual solvents. Results indicate that the absorption rate of CO2 into the blends and the pure SG solution was one order magnitude higher than that into the [Bmim]BF4 solution. Compared with the pure SG solution, the blend with the same amount of SG exhibited a higher absorption rate of CO2. The CO2 absorption rate into the blends was 40% higher than that into the pure SG solution under the same tested condition. Moreover, the CO2 absorption into the blends was 20% higher than the sum of that into the individual solvent, indicating that the [Bmim]BF4 facilitated the CO2 absorption into the SG. Our previous work revealed that the [Bmim]BF4 had a base catalytic effect on the hydration of CO2 (Lu et al., 2013). Meanwhile, the [Bmim]BF4 may be also served as a base catalysis for the deprotonation of the zwitterion.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For practical engineering application, the temperature of the flue gas after the wet flue gas desulfurization units is about 40-50 ◦ C. The temperature swing between the rich solution from absorption column and the regenerated lean solution from the stripping column will impact the energy consumption of the heat exchange. Meanwhile, the temperature will have a great impact on the CO2 absorption based on the Arrhenius equation. An appropriate absorption temperature is essential to achieve the great CO2 absorption performance and the low energy consumption of the heat exchange.
texts.text: For practical engineering application, the temperature of the flue gas after the wet flue gas desulfurization units is about 40-50 ◦ C. The temperature swing between the rich solution from absorption column and the regenerated lean solution from the stripping column will impact the energy consumption of the heat exchange. Meanwhile, the temperature will have a great impact on the CO2 absorption based on the Arrhenius equation. An appropriate absorption temperature is essential to achieve the great CO2 absorption performance and the low energy consumption of the heat exchange.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 4 shows the CO2 absorption rate as a function of temperature. As expected, the apparent CO2 absorption rate was accelerated as the absorption temperature increased. For example, the apparent CO2 absorption rate increased from 0.5 to 0.9 mmol min -1 when the absorption temperature increased from 30 to 60 ◦ C. According to Arrhenius formula, the reaction rate constant increases with the temperature up. And the same conclusion was drawn in some other investigations (Dash et al., 2011). However, it does not mean that the higher temperature operates at, the higher absorption rate achieves, because the solubility of CO2 in the solution decreases at elevated temperature (Majer et al., 2008) which lowers down the driving force of CO2 absorption.
texts.text: Fig. 4 shows the CO2 absorption rate as a function of temperature. As expected, the apparent CO2 absorption rate was accelerated as the absorption temperature increased. For example, the apparent CO2 absorption rate increased from 0.5 to 0.9 mmol min -1 when the absorption temperature increased from 30 to 60 ◦ C. According to Arrhenius formula, the reaction rate constant increases with the temperature up. And the same conclusion was drawn in some other investigations (Dash et al., 2011). However, it does not mean that the higher temperature operates at, the higher absorption rate achieves, because the solubility of CO2 in the solution decreases at elevated temperature (Majer et al., 2008) which lowers down the driving force of CO2 absorption.

texts.orig: from 0.30 to 0.48 mmol min -1 when the inlet CO2 concentration increased from 5 to 15% (v/v). The CO2 absorption rate was determined by the mass transfer rate of CO2 from gas to liquid phase and the chemical reaction rate between SG and CO2. Further the increase of the CO2 concentration from 15 to 20% (v/v) did not remarkably increase the CO2 absorption rate, indicating the apparent CO2 absorption rate may be limited by the chemical reaction rate at high CO2 concentration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In general, flue gas contains 3-8% (v/v) oxygen. For benchmark 5 M MEA solution, the oxidative degradation rate caused by oxygen was about 7.4 mM h -1 (Chi and Rochelle, 2002). The degradation of solvent not only decreases the CO2 absorption rate and capacity but also increases the operation cost of CO2 capture. The effect of O2 on the CO2 absorption rate was investigated at 30 ◦ C. As shown in Fig. 6, the CO2 absorption rate decreased with the increase of the oxygen. An increase of oxygen from 3 to 8% (v/v) resulted in a decrease of the CO2 absorption rate by 25%. For MEA solution, at the same testing condition, the CO2 absorption rate decreased 50% (Lu et al., 2013). Results revealed that the SG and [Bmim]BF4 blends possessed a greater anti-oxidative ability compared with MEA solution.
texts.text: In general, flue gas contains 3-8% (v/v) oxygen. For benchmark 5 M MEA solution, the oxidative degradation rate caused by oxygen was about 7.4 mM h -1 (Chi and Rochelle, 2002). The degradation of solvent not only decreases the CO2 absorption rate and capacity but also increases the operation cost of CO2 capture. The effect of O2 on the CO2 absorption rate was investigated at 30 ◦ C. As shown in Fig. 6, the CO2 absorption rate decreased with the increase of the oxygen. An increase of oxygen from 3 to 8% (v/v) resulted in a decrease of the CO2 absorption rate by 25%. For MEA solution, at the same testing condition, the CO2 absorption rate decreased 50% (Lu et al., 2013). Results revealed that the SG and [Bmim]BF4 blends possessed a greater anti-oxidative ability compared with MEA solution.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In general, the CO2-laden solvent will be regenerated under elevated temperature after absorption. The generated solvent will be sent back to absorption column for CO2 absorption. The stripped CO2 will be purified and compressed for CO2 sequestration. For the MEA process, it has been confirmed that portion of MEA will evaporate and degrade during regeneration, resulting in an increase of the operation cost (Dash et al., 2011; Abu-Zahra et al., 2007). The regeneration of the blend (0.8 M SG and 0.2 M [Bmim]BF4) and 1 M pure SG was investigated under 102 ◦ C. The regenerated solutions were reused for CO2 absorption. The regeneration efficiency was defined as:
texts.text: In general, the CO2-laden solvent will be regenerated under elevated temperature after absorption. The generated solvent will be sent back to absorption column for CO2 absorption. The stripped CO2 will be purified and compressed for CO2 sequestration. For the MEA process, it has been confirmed that portion of MEA will evaporate and degrade during regeneration, resulting in an increase of the operation cost (Dash et al., 2011; Abu-Zahra et al., 2007). The regeneration of the blend (0.8 M SG and 0.2 M [Bmim]BF4) and 1 M pure SG was investigated under 102 ◦ C. The regenerated solutions were reused for CO2 absorption. The regeneration efficiency was defined as:

texts.orig: Fig. 7. Determination of regeneration efficiency as a function of the regeneration cycles. (CO2 absorption at 30 ◦ C, CO2 regeneration at 102 ◦ C, volume of the solvent: 250 mL, 1.0 mol L -1 SG and 0.8 mol L -1 SG + 0.2 mol L -1 [Bmim]BF4.)
texts.text: Fig. 7. Determination of regeneration efficiency as a function of the regeneration cycles. (CO2 absorption at 30 ◦ C, CO2 regeneration at 102 ◦ C, volume of the solvent: 250 mL, 1.0 mol L -1 SG and 0.8 mol L -1 SG + 0.2 mol L -1 [Bmim]BF4.)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: cycle of regeneration, the blend lost 19% of CO2 absorption capacity compared with the fresh counterparts. The pure SG solution lost 23% of CO2 absorption capacity after first cycle of regeneration, indicating that the blends were easier to be regenerated compared with the pure SG solution. This can be attributed to the portion of the physical absorption by the ionic liquid in the blends. The regeneration efficiency decreased with the increase of the regeneration cycles. After three cycles, the blend retained 64% of the initial CO2 absorption capacity while the pure SG solution only possessed 54% of the initial CO2 absorption capacity. Since all the absorbed CO2 was released in the regeneration process (data not shown), the drop in the regeneration efficiency with numbers of cycles was due to the degradation of the solvent at the high regeneration temperature. The high regeneration efficiency can reduce the sensible heat and hence decrease the energy consumption for CO2 absorption.
texts.text: cycle of regeneration, the blend lost 19% of CO2 absorption capacity compared with the fresh counterparts. The pure SG solution lost 23% of CO2 absorption capacity after first cycle of regeneration, indicating that the blends were easier to be regenerated compared with the pure SG solution. This can be attributed to the portion of the physical absorption by the ionic liquid in the blends. The regeneration efficiency decreased with the increase of the regeneration cycles. After three cycles, the blend retained 64% of the initial CO2 absorption capacity while the pure SG solution only possessed 54% of the initial CO2 absorption capacity. Since all the absorbed CO2 was released in the regeneration process (data not shown), the drop in the regeneration efficiency with numbers of cycles was due to the degradation of the solvent at the high regeneration temperature. The high regeneration efficiency can reduce the sensible heat and hence decrease the energy consumption for CO2 absorption.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, a blend of an amino acid (SG) and an ionic liquid ([Bmim]BF4) was proposed to reduce the energy penalty of CO2 capture. The CO2 absorption rate and the regeneration efficiency of the blends were investigated. Results revealed that the CO2 absorption rate achieved the highest with the blending mole ratio of 8:2 for the SG and [Bmim]BF4. As expected, the CO2 absorption rate of the blends increased with the increase of the absorption temperature and CO2 partial pressure. For the practical application, a suitable temperature should be selected based on the tradeoff between the CO2 absorption rate and the solvent degradation rate. Moreover, the blends exhibited a better antioxidant ability compared with the benchmark MEA solution. Compared with the pure SG solution, the blends possessed a higher regeneration efficiency which may reduce the sensible heat of CO2 capture. Overall, the blends of amino acid and the ionic liquid may be an alternative to the MEA solution to reduce the energy consumption for CO2 capture.
texts.text: In this work, a blend of an amino acid (SG) and an ionic liquid ([Bmim]BF4) was proposed to reduce the energy penalty of CO2 capture. The CO2 absorption rate and the regeneration efficiency of the blends were investigated. Results revealed that the CO2 absorption rate achieved the highest with the blending mole ratio of 8:2 for the SG and [Bmim]BF4. As expected, the CO2 absorption rate of the blends increased with the increase of the absorption temperature and CO2 partial pressure. For the practical application, a suitable temperature should be selected based on the tradeoff between the CO2 absorption rate and the solvent degradation rate. Moreover, the blends exhibited a better antioxidant ability compared with the benchmark MEA solution. Compared with the pure SG solution, the blends possessed a higher regeneration efficiency which may reduce the sensible heat of CO2 capture. Overall, the blends of amino acid and the ionic liquid may be an alternative to the MEA solution to reduce the energy consumption for CO2 capture.
